questions.question: Why does a single agent fail at broad research tasks?
options: Single agents cannot use search tools
It reads sources sequentially, fills half its context with raw text, forgets early sources by the last one, and cannot parallelize
Research requires at least three GPUs
Single agents are rate-limited harder
questions.explanation: The supervisor pattern fixes this: a lead plans and synthesizes while workers each get a fresh 200k window for one narrow sub-question, running in parallel. The lead never sees the raw papers — only worker summaries.

questions.question: What did Anthropic's measurements reveal about WHY multi-agent research wins?
options: Larger models are always better
+90.2% over single-agent Opus 4 on research evals — and 80% of BrowseComp variance is explained by token usage alone, so fresh context per subagent is the main mechanism
It wins only because of better prompts
Parallelism alone explains everything
questions.explanation: The headline mechanism isn't cleverness — it's context budget. Each Sonnet worker gets its own window instead of sharing the lead's. Specialized prompts and parallel wall-clock are the secondary mechanisms.

questions.question: What does "scale effort to query complexity" mean operationally?
options: Always use the maximum number of agents
The LEAD estimates: simple queries get one agent and 3-10 tool calls; complex ones get 10+ agents — and the caller shouldn't have to decide
Use bigger models for longer queries
Charge users by query length
questions.explanation: Multi-agent costs ~15x the tokens of single-agent, so spawning workers for a simple lookup wastes money. The lead's first job is estimating whether decomposition pays — broad sub-questions first, deeper workers only where warranted.

questions.question: Why does Anthropic use rainbow deployments for its Research system?
options: For visual branding of release stages
Agents are long-running and stateful, so blue-green cutover would kill in-flight runs — rainbow gradually rolls in new versions while old ones drain
To A/B test seven model versions at once
Rainbow is cheaper than blue-green
questions.explanation: Traditional deployment assumes stateless request handlers. A multi-hour research run can't be cut over mid-flight, so version rollout must let existing trajectories complete on their original version.

questions.question: What is the worst synthesis failure mode when two workers return contradictory facts?
options: Adding an extra round to re-ask
Silently picking one side — the user never learns a disagreement existed; the lead must re-ask or surface the conflict explicitly
Reporting both answers
Asking a third worker to vote
questions.explanation: Re-asking costs a round; noting the disagreement costs honesty points; silent resolution destroys trust invisibly. The lesson also flags lead-hallucinated plans and worker over-exploration as the other production failure modes.
